From this, it ‘s clear that the author of Phaladeepika held the Hora- sara in high esteem ; and this fact redounds to the glory of the present work.

This work purports to be written by no less a person than Prithuyasas, on of the celebrated astronomer of Ujjain, viz, Warahamihira; for in the colophon at the end of every Chapter, we read that this 2 work has been written by Prithuyasas, son of Varaha- mihira, Apart from this, we do not find any statement or assertion anywhere in the body of the text, that! this
work is composed by Prithuyasas, son. of Varahamihira — as isfound in the very first verse of the FATT S—

But this omis- sion will not stand in the way of deciding the authorship of this work, for there are other evidences to show that this work must have been written by the “same Prithuyasas* It is well known that Varahamihira was a great devotee of the Sun-God.

His son, likewise was a staunch worshipper of the same Deity, as is evidenced by the benedictory verse of the sagatfaart In the present work too, the author pays his devout homage to the Sun on the model of Varahamihira See how the benedictory verse of this work—

D [he object of Pri- thuyasas in writing this work is to make the abstruse Hora Sastra intelligible to persons of average intelligence, One may put the question here as to why this 13 neces~ sary, when his father’himself has already simplified and abridged it in his gate According to the present: author, even this *G tas was not within easy ‘reach or understan ling of orliniry m:n.
to supplement his father’s great work.
Just like his father, Prithuyasas too is not in favour of two or more planets combining in a single house.
Unlike his father, he gives the effects of the conjunction of three and four planets also.

The Dasa of an asterism in which a malefic is situated will cause a good deal of trouble to the native at a period which is proportional to the distance travelled by the lord of the Dasa in the star he is in.

Similarly, when the twelve houses are divided into three groups of four Rasis each, if there is a weak malefic without benefic aspect in the first group and if the native is passing through the Dasa of that planet in the initial stage of his life then the end of this Dasa will prove fatal to him.
The birth of a son in the same star as that of the father is dangerous to the mother, Similarly, the identity of stars between the mother and the son is harmful to the father.
According to him, the Ashtavarga Dasa system is the best of all.

In Chapter XVIII also, he gives the following valuable information: A person will be married during the sub-period of the planet owning the Navamsa of the lord of the 7th house in the person’s birth-chart.
The death of a brother may take place when Saturn passes thro’ the Rasi indicated by the sumstotal of the. figures of the lords of the 6th, 8th and 12th houses.
One may quit the world during the Dasa of the planet presiding over the Navamsa in which Rahu is posited.

Similarly, the month of death is to be found out by multiplying the Sun’s figure in minutes by that of Rahu (in minutes) and dividing the product by 21,600 and then by adding this quotient to the Sun’s figure, which will indicate some Rasi.

Slokas 20-23.—By nature, the Sun is dark-red in colour; the Moon, white; Mars, deep-red; Mercury, of the colour of Durva grass (green); Jupiter, of golden hue; Venus, white; Saturn, blue (black); Ketu, brown (mud-coloured); Mandi or Gulika, multi-coloured; and Rahu resembles the dark collyrium.

Though the colour assigned to a Rasi is pleasant or good, it assumes the shade of the planet it is conjoined with A planet not conjoined with another, when posited in his own Kshetra or Amsa will reveal fully his natural qualities (such as colour, etc.); if, on the other hand, he be posited in another’s Rasi (or Amsa) or conjoined with another planet, he would have his natural colour influenced by (mixed with) those of the Rasi or the planet as the case may be,

translation: Slokas 1-2.—The Sun is said to be strong when he is in the Northerly course (Uttarayana), when he is posited in his own house, his exaltation Rasi, Navamsa; Drekkana or Hora, He is also strong in his own day (Sunday).
He is strong when posited in the initial portion of a Rasi; of medium strength in the middle of a Rasi and weak at the end of it.

~ Blokas = 49-51.—When the Lagna or its Amsa belongs to Jupiter, the departed person goes to heaven’.
If the Lagna or its Amsa belong to Venus or the’ Moon; the native goes to the world of the Manes.
If the Lagna or its Amsa be owned by the Sun or Mars, the native goes to the world of Mortals.
If Ketu occupy or own the Lagna Rasi or its Amsa, the departed will be in Hell.
If the Lagna or its Amsa belong to Mercury; he will become an animal of the brutal kind.
If the Lagna or its Amsa belong to Saturn or Rahu, the native will be reborn in the world of Devils or Manes.
